followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the overall verdict and score essentially unchanged. The other reviews strengthened my view that reliance on companion preprints and homogeneity restrictions should be made explicit. They did not remove my main action-level concern about whether Omega uniquely determines the full Wald entropy for the advertised broad class.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Treating the HTML equation-rendering failure as a technical objection to the paper is not appropriate; it limits review confidence but is not a mathematical flaw in the manuscript itself.
The claim that the Wald reduction is simply well supported is too strong; it bypasses the action-level and uplift ambiguities that determine Noether-charge entropy.

A broad statement that 2D boundary terms must contribute to Wald entropy is too blunt. The sharper issue is the usual Noether-charge ambiguity from total derivatives/topological terms and the need to fix a representative action.

The specific G_5-sector objection is plausible as a warning but under-argued as stated; the paper's deeper problem is not one named Horndeski sector alone, but whether the d-dimensional entropy tensor is fully captured by the reduced h4 coefficient across all allowed parents.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The reliance on companion preprints for reconstruction, integrability, and Birkhoff results is a real limitation of stand-alone auditability.
The simple/nondegenerate horizon assumption should be explicit, especially for regular black holes with extremal endpoints.
The generalized Smarr formula requires homogeneity assumptions and should be presented as a subclass result.
The compact hyperbolic horizon case raises a plausible symmetric-criticality subtlety requiring specialist checking.
The identification of the integration constant with physical mass needs normalization/asymptotic-charge input beyond the formal integrated equation.

main_claims.claim: Any static black hole of the form ds^2=-f(r)dt^2+dr^2/f(r)+r^2 dSigma^2 with suitable horizon topology can be regarded as a vacuum solution of an extended quasi-topological gravity via an integrable 2D dilaton theory.
main_claims.notes: The claim is largely inherited from cited reconstruction and Birkhoff results rather than proved in this letter. Other reviewers correctly flagged this reliance. The paper also requires restrictions such as invertible mass dependence, regularity/differentiability, and nondegenerate branches that are not always foregrounded in the headline formulation.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Extended quasi-topological gravities, especially around eqs. (1), (21)-(24), and (30)-(34).

main_claims.claim: The integrated equation Omega(r,f)=M identifies the generating-function constant with the thermodynamic mass and gives M=Omega(r_+) at the horizon.
main_claims.notes: Within a fixed normalization and a nondegenerate branch, this follows cleanly from the level-curve equation. The GR/AdS normalization check supports the interpretation, but a boundary/charge normalization condition remains implicit for generic non-polynomial reconstructed theories.
main_claims.paper_location: Eqs. (23)-(24), (35), and GR/AdS check in eqs. (25)-(29).

main_claims.claim: The Hawking temperature is T=-(1/4pi)(partial_{r_+} Omega)/(partial_f Omega) evaluated at f=0.
main_claims.notes: The formula follows by differentiating Omega(r,f)=M along the branch and using surface gravity. It requires a simple nonextremal horizon and partial_f Omega nonzero; other reviewers' nondegenerate-horizon caveat is valid.

main_claims.claim: The Wald entropy for the full d-dimensional gravitational theory can be computed from the reduced 2D Lagrangian and equals S=-4 pi integral dr_+ partial_f Omega(r_+).
main_claims.notes: The derivation is plausible for a fixed representative reduced action where the relevant reduced Lagrangian coefficient h4 is known. It is not fully justified for the advertised broad class of parent theories, especially if different d-dimensional uplifts or action ambiguities can share the same reduced equations but alter the Noether charge.

main_claims.claim: A generalized first law and Smarr relation with pressure and dimensionful-coupling work terms follow for homogeneous subclasses.
main_claims.notes: The scaling argument is standard and credible for the explicit ansatz (59)-(61). Gemini's homogeneity caveat is valid: this is a subclass result and should not be read as automatic for arbitrary phenomenological metrics reconstructed into the framework.

main_claims.claim: For the d=4 AdS Bardeen-type example, the proposed formulas produce explicit M, T, and S satisfying the fixed-coupling first law and reducing to the area law as gamma -> 0.
main_claims.notes: The displayed mass and temperature are algebraically consistent with the horizon equation, and the entropy integral appears consistent up to additive normalization. A human should still spot-check the global regularity/topology interpretation and the sign/topology convention.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A fixed representative of the d-dimensional parent action, with topological/boundary ambiguities and the integration constant in h4 fixed.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Reconstruction discussion around eqs. (30)-(34); Wald entropy eqs. (44)-(46).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Wald entropy is a property of an action, not only of its solution-generating equation. Without this choice, Omega may determine a first-law derivative but not the absolute Wald entropy.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The horizon is nonextremal and simple, with partial_f Omega(r_+,0) nonzero and f'(r_+)>0 on the branch considered.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The temperature formula and entropy integration rely on these quantities being regular and on a smooth Euclidean cone.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The mass-radius relation is locally invertible when using r_+ as the thermodynamic coordinate.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Abstract and reconstruction paragraph before eq. (30); first-law derivation eqs. (35)-(48).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Regular black holes can have multiple branches or extremal endpoints; the one-variable first-law derivation is branch-local.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Symmetric criticality and the reduction-to-variation equivalence hold for all stated horizon topologies and boundary conditions, including compact hyperbolic quotients.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Paragraph invoking symmetric criticality before eq. (12).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper uses reduced 2D equations and the reduced Lagrangian to infer d-dimensional equations and entropy; this requires the reduction principle to apply without hidden quotient or boundary subtleties.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The functions h_i and Omega are sufficiently differentiable, and the entropy integral is finite on the relevant parameter range.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The formulas involve partial derivatives and indefinite integrals that can fail at singular branches, phase-transition points, or nonanalytic regular black-hole models.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The generalized Smarr formula is being asserted only for homogeneous subclasses or for choices of scaling weights that make the mass homogeneous.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Euler's theorem does not apply to an arbitrary reconstructed phenomenological metric unless the dependence on couplings and length scales has the required homogeneity.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the reconstruction theorem in Borissova (2026) guarantee a parent d-dimensional action whose Wald entropy is uniquely captured by the reduced h4 coefficient?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The present paper cites the theorem but does not reproduce enough of it to verify uniqueness of the parent action or behavior of Noether charges.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: For parent actions with curvature-derivative or non-polynomial curvature invariants, does the Iyer-Wald entropy tensor reduce exactly to the partial_R expression used in eqs. (40)-(44)?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires checking off-ansatz variations of the full covariant action, not just the displayed reduced equations.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are topological terms, total derivatives, and low-dimensional Euler/Gauss-Bonnet-type additions intentionally excluded or fixed by convention?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Such terms can alter Wald entropy or its normalization while leaving local equations unchanged, and the paper does not specify an entropy-normalization convention beyond examples.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the principle of symmetric criticality apply without qualification to compact hyperbolic horizon quotients in the reduction used here?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is a group-theoretic and variational-reduction issue requiring careful comparison with the hypotheses of Palais/Fels-Torre-type theorems.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the Bardeen-AdS example's topology/sign convention and claimed regularity globally correct, not just algebraically consistent at the level of f(r)?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The local formulas can be checked algebraically, but global regularity and horizon topology require domain expertise and possibly the PDF equations.

novelty_assessment: The useful novelty is the thermodynamic packaging: mass, temperature, entropy, and work terms are expressed directly through a generating function Omega for a broad g_tt g_rr=-1 class. The hard reconstruction and integrability machinery appears mostly imported from very recent companion work by the author and collaborators, and related entropy computations in polynomial/non-polynomial quasi-topological gravities are cited. I therefore see the contribution as a promising unification and extension rather than a field-shaping standalone theorem. The other reviewers' higher novelty/significance scores are reasonable if the companion reconstruction theorem is accepted, but the present letter should more sharply separate inherited results from new thermodynamic consequences.
score_rationale: The framework is promising and the local thermodynamic derivations are coherent for a fixed action representative, fixed couplings, and a nonextremal branch. The score remains limited by unresolved action-level issues around Wald entropy, reliance on companion reconstruction theorems, and implicit branch/homogeneity assumptions. Other reviewers raised useful caveats but did not justify raising the technical-soundness score; conversely, the HTML-rendering problem should not count as a paper flaw.
summary: The paper proposes a compact thermodynamic framework for static d-dimensional black holes with g_tt g_rr=-1 by encoding the solution in an integrable two-dimensional dilaton/Horndeski reduction with generating function Omega. Its main outputs are M=Omega(r_+), a temperature formula from the level curve Omega(r,f)=M, an entropy formula S=-4 pi integral dr_+ partial_f Omega interpreted as Wald entropy, and extensions to pressure/coupling work terms plus a Bardeen-AdS example. After reading the other reviews, I still view the local thermodynamic algebra as promising but not fully action-level certified: the main unresolved issue is whether the full d-dimensional Wald entropy is uniquely and generally determined by the reduced generating function for the very broad extended-QTG class claimed.

technical_objections.evidence: Equation (34) defines h4 through an integral representation tied to a particular reconstruction. Additions to the action that leave the reduced equations or Omega unchanged can shift Wald entropy by topological or ambiguity terms. Since Wald entropy is an action-level object, the paper should specify the representative d-dimensional parent action or a convention fixing these ambiguities.
technical_objections.objection: The central entropy formula is not uniquely determined by Omega unless a specific representative action and entropy normalization are fixed.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper argues that the reduced Lagrangian is linear in the 2D Ricci scalar and has no curvature derivatives, so the entropy tensor contraction reduces to a partial derivative. But the parent theories are described broadly enough to include non-polynomial curvature and curvature-derivative invariants. It is not shown in this letter that the full d-dimensional variational derivative with respect to Riemann commutes with symmetry reduction or that off-ansatz curvature components cannot contribute to the binormal contraction.
technical_objections.objection: The reduction of the full d-dimensional Wald tensor to a partial derivative of the reduced 2D Lagrangian is asserted too quickly for the advertised general class.

technical_objections.evidence: Other reviewers correctly note that the integrability condition, Birkhoff theorem, and reconstruction formulas are stated rather than proved here. This is acceptable for a short letter if accurately scoped, but it weakens stand-alone confidence in broad phrases such as 'any such black hole'.
technical_objections.objection: The reconstruction and Birkhoff claims are used as load-bearing inputs but are mostly deferred to companion preprints.
technical_objections.paper_location: Extended quasi-topological gravities section; reconstruction paragraph before eq. (30); citations to Borissova (2026), Borissova and Carballo-Rubio (2026a,b), Boyanov and Carballo-Rubio (2025), and Carballo-Rubio (2026).

technical_objections.evidence: Equations (35), (37), and (47) vary r_+ while holding the theory data fixed. Once Lambda or other dimensionful scales enter Omega, the mass and entropy can vary with those scales and work terms are required. The paper later discusses this, but eq. (48) should be explicitly labeled as fixed-coupling.
technical_objections.objection: The first law in eq. (48) is proved only for variations along a fixed-theory, fixed-coupling one-parameter horizon branch.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper assumes an outer zero of f, but the temperature formula divides by partial_f Omega and the Euclidean argument assumes a smooth nonextremal cone. Extremal horizons, multiple roots, and noninvertible M(r_+) branches are common in regular black-hole models and require separate treatment.
technical_objections.objection: Degenerate horizons and branches with partial_f Omega(r_+,0)=0 are not handled.
technical_objections.paper_location: Thermodynamics section, assumptions before eq. (35), and eq. (37).

technical_objections.evidence: The text invokes symmetric criticality for compact symmetry groups while also allowing compact hyperbolic horizons. A compact hyperbolic quotient has finite volume but is locally modeled on a noncompact continuous isometry group, so a specialist should check whether the cited theorem applies exactly as used.
technical_objections.objection: The principle-of-symmetric-criticality step may need an explicit caveat for compact hyperbolic horizon quotients.
technical_objections.paper_location: Paragraph invoking symmetric criticality before eq. (12).

technical_objections.evidence: The text says tau is periodically identified with period kappa/2pi, while the inverse temperature should be 2pi/kappa. The subsequent formula T=f'(r_+)/4pi is correct, so this appears to be a wording error rather than a derivational failure.
technical_objections.objection: There is a local inversion/typo in the Euclidean-period statement.
